summary: Joel shares a brief update linking to his CityDesk development series about dealing with buggy third-party code libraries. He also recommends a Harvard philosophy professor's guide on writing, arguing that its advice about writing for a 'lazy, stupid, and mean' reader is the best guidance programmers can follow when designing and documenting their code.
key_points: Third-party code dependencies can introduce crashing bugs that are unacceptable in your own shipped product
Good writing advice from other disciplines (philosophy) applies directly to programming documentation
Writers should assume their reader is lazy, stupid, and mean — meaning they won't work to understand you, need simple explanations, and will interpret ambiguity uncharitably
money_quotes: For the umpteenth time, I found myself dependent on a code library which had a crashing bug that was unacceptable in code I shipped.
What are you supposed to do if you're the chef at Les Halles and your fishmonger is giving you smelly fish?
Pretend that your reader is lazy, stupid, and mean.
He's lazy in that he doesn't want to figure out what your convoluted sentences are supposed to mean, and he doesn't want to figure out what your argument is, if it's not already obvious.
I can think of no better advice for the kind of writing that programmers should be doing in designing and documenting their code.
key_insight: Programmers should write documentation and design specs as if their reader is lazy, stupid, and mean — making arguments obvious, explanations simple, and language unambiguous.
